1: Hannah's prayer (the Magnificat of the OT) is dated to ca. 1100 BCE in the narrative framework and represents one of the oldest poetic compositions preserved in Samuel. Its closest structural parallel is Psalm 113 (the praise of YHWH who raises the poor from the dust). The prayer's theological structure — YHWH reverses human fortunes by humbling the proud and exalting the lowly — is the dominant theological pattern of the Samuel books: Saul is humbled, David is exalted; the priest Eli's house falls, Samuel rises. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) is a conscious typological expansion of Hannah's prayer: the same reversal theology, the same vocabulary of the hungry being filled and the rich sent empty away, the same divine agent (YHWH/the Lord) as the initiator of the reversal. Placing Hannah's prayer in its canonical context shows it as the theological overture to both the Samuel books and the NT Advent narratives.
12: Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas are described as bənê bəliyyaʿal — 'sons of worthlessness/Belial' — the same phrase used for the men of Gibeah (Judges 19:22) and for Nabal (1 Samuel 25:17, 25). The compound bəliyyaʿal (bəlî = without + yōʿal = profit/worth) designates persons who operate entirely outside covenant norms — those from whom no positive covenant contribution can be expected. The specific sins enumerated: taking the meat by force before the fat has been offered to YHWH (vv13-16), and lying with the women who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting (v22). Both sins are direct inversions of priestly vocation: the priest is to burn the fat first (Leviticus 3:16-17), and the sanctuary is to be protected from sexual defilement (cf. the Zimri-Cozbi incident, Num 25). The Shiloh priesthood's corruption is the institutional background that makes Samuel's emergence and eventually the royal institution both necessary and theologically justified.
27: The oracle of the 'man of God' (ʾîš hāʾelōhîm) against Eli's house introduces a motif that will recur throughout the DH: the prophetic word that announces the end of a dynasty or institution, followed by the fulfillment in the narrative. The formula hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm ('behold, days are coming') is the standard prophetic announcement formula for future judgment (it appears 16 times in Jeremiah alone). The judgment oracle against the house of Eli (vv31-36) announces: (1) cutting off (kārat) the strength of Eli's father's house; (2) no old man in his house forever; (3) a faithful priest (kōhēn neʾĕmān) to replace him, 'who will do according to what is in my heart and in my soul.' The 'faithful priest' of v35 is variously identified as Samuel, Zadok (who replaces Abiathar/Eli's line, 1 Kings 2:27,35), or eschatologically as Christ. The oracle's immediate fulfillment in the deaths of Hophni and Phinehas (1 Samuel 4) and its long fulfillment in Zadok's appointment makes this a paradigmatic example of prophetic word-and-fulfillment theology.
35: The 'faithful priest' (kōhēn neʾĕmān) oracle is the pivot of the Shiloh narrative. The terms neʾĕmān (faithful, reliable, established — from the ʾāmēn root) echo the Davidic covenant vocabulary of 2 Samuel 7:16 (wᵉneʾĕman bêtəḵā, 'your house shall be established/faithful forever'). The phrase 'a sure (neʾĕmān) house' appears in both the Eli oracle (v35) and the Nathan oracle (2 Samuel 7:16), creating a structural parallel: what is denied to Eli's house (a sure/faithful house) is granted to David's house. The theological logic: the priestly failure at Shiloh clears the ground for the Davidic institution — Eli's corruption is not merely personal but institutional, requiring not just a better priest but a new covenantal arrangement whose embodiment will eventually be the priestly-royal Messiah.
2: “There is no one holy like the LORD (ein qadosh kaYHWH); there is no one besides you (ki ein biltekha); there is no rock (tzur) like our God.” The rock imagery is the divine-warrior epithet of the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:4, 15, 18, 30-31). Hannah’s song echoes Mosaic theology deliberately. The incomparability formula (ein...ki...ein) became the structural backbone of Deutero-Isaiah’s monotheistic polemic: “Is there a God besides me? There is no Rock; I know not any” (Isaiah 44:8). Both YHWH’s uniqueness and his rock-character point to absolute reliability — the ground of Hannah’s confidence in a world that seemed to offer her nothing.
3: “The LORD is a God of knowledge (el de’ot), and by him deeds are weighed (ve-lo nitkenû ’alilot).” The plural de’ot (knowledges) is unique to this text — it implies comprehensive omniscience from all angles simultaneously. The weighing of deeds recalls the Egyptian maat (truth-balance) concept but roots it in YHWH’s personal assessment. The verse is a theological counter to Peninnah’s boasting: pride in fertility will be exposed when YHWH’s comprehensive assessment is applied.
4: “The bows of the mighty (gibborim) are shattered (chatim), but those who stumbled (nikhshalim) are girded with strength.” The first of the song’s reversal pairs. Military might is broken while the weak are empowered. Psalms 46:9 (“he breaks the bow and shatters the spear”) participates in the same divine-warrior-reversal theology. Luke 1:51-52 (Magnificat) quotes this logic directly: “He has scattered the proud... he has brought down the mighty from their thrones.”
5: “The barren woman has borne seven (aqarah yaldah shivah), but she who has many children languishes (ummelet).” The seven-children image is the fullness of the reversal: the most barren woman now has the most fertile outcome. The number seven signals completeness. Hannah herself will bear five more children after Samuel (1 Samuel 2:21), totaling six — the “seven” may be hyperbolic or symbolic. Mary’s Magnificat (Luke 1:53: “he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty”) translates Hannah’s reversal-logic into the vocabulary of the new creation.
6: “The LORD brings death and gives life (YHWH memit umechayyeh); he brings down to Sheol and raises up.” YHWH’s sovereign authority extends over the boundary between life and death. Deuteronomy 32:39 uses almost identical language: “I put to death and I bring to life.” This is the theological foundation for later resurrection hope: if YHWH commands both death and life, neither is a permanent condition beyond his reach. 1 Samuel 2:6 is the OT root of what Paul develops in 1 Corinthians 15:26 (“the last enemy to be destroyed is death”) and what Jesus claims in John 11:25 (“I am the resurrection and the life”). Hannah sings resurrection theology before Israel has a vocabulary for it.
7: “The LORD makes poor and makes rich (YHWH morish uma’ashir); he brings low, and he also lifts up.” Economic and social status are within YHWH’s sovereign disposition. James 4:10 (“humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will lift you up”) and 1 Peter 5:6 echo this logic. The Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-10) are structured on this same schema: blessed are the poor, the mourners, the meek — those at the bottom of each social axis.
8: “He raises the poor from the dust (meqim me’afar dal) and lifts the needy from the ash heap, to seat them with princes and give them a throne of honor.” The dust-to-throne reversal is the paradigmatic elevation narrative: Joseph from prison to Pharaoh’s right hand, David from pasture to palace. The ashpot (dunghill) imagery denotes the most abject poverty (cf. Psalms 113:7, which quotes this verse nearly verbatim). The verse concludes with cosmic grounding: “for the pillars of the earth are YHWH’s, and on them he has set the world.” Social reversals are possible because YHWH is the cosmic architect whose jurisdiction is total.
9: “He will guard the feet of his faithful ones (chasidim), but the wicked will be silenced in darkness, for not by strength does a man prevail (ki-lo vechoach yigbar ish).” The chasidim (faithful ones, covenant loyal) are YHWH’s protected community. The climactic claim — “not by strength does a man prevail” — is the theological summary of the entire reversal poem: human power is not the decisive variable in history. YHWH’s sovereign ordering determines outcomes.
10: The song’s climax: “The LORD will judge the ends of the earth; he will give strength to his king (melekho) and lift high the horn of his anointed (meshicho).” Remarkable: there was no king in Israel when Hannah sang this. The monarchy has not yet been established. Hannah speaks of a coming anointed king as part of YHWH’s eschatological action. This is the first occurrence of mashiach (anointed one) in the narrative books, entirely prospective. The song anticipates the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7), the Messianic psalms (Psalms 2:2; 1 Samuel 18:50; 1 Samuel 89:51), and ultimately the identity of Jesus as Christos (the Greek translation of mashiach).
11: Elkanah returned to Ramah while Samuel remained at Shiloh to minister to YHWH (meshareth et-YHWH) in the presence of Eli. The term shereth (minister) is the standard term for Levitical and priestly service at the sanctuary (Numbers 18:2; Deuteronomy 10:8). The scene’s pathos is understated: the family is permanently divided by the child’s consecration. Samuel’s service begins immediately upon his dedication.
13: The priests’ corrupt practice is detailed: when someone offered a sacrifice, the priest’s servant would come with a three-pronged fork (mazleg shelosh hashinnayim) while the meat was boiling and take whatever the fork brought up. The shelamim (peace offering) procedure in Leviticus 7:28-36 specified that the breast and right thigh went to the priest — a specific, limited portion. Eli’s sons circumvent this by taking from the communal pot before the worshiper’s portion was served, an unspecified random amount. This is theft from the sacred meal that was intended to foster communion between YHWH, the priest, and the worshiper.
14: The fork was thrust indiscriminately into whatever vessel — pan, kettle, cauldron, or pot — and whatever came up was taken. The variety of cooking vessels mentioned suggests different parts of the sanctuary kitchen were affected. The practice was applied to “all the Israelites who came to Shiloh” — systemic, not occasional. The systemic exploitation of the sanctuary economy parallels the temple money-changers Jesus encounters in John 2:14-16: what should facilitate encounter with God has been turned into a mechanism for priestly enrichment.
15: The sons’ behavior escalates: they demanded raw meat even before the fat (chelev) was burned. The fat was YHWH’s portion — Leviticus 3:16 mandates “all the fat belongs to the LORD” as “an everlasting statute.” Demanding raw meat before the divine portion inverts the entire sacrificial order: the worshiper’s appetites precede YHWH’s honor. This is not merely liturgical carelessness but theological inversion — prioritizing human appetites over divine honor in the very act designed to express the reverse.
16: When a worshiper tried to protest, the response was coercive: “No, hand it over now; if not, I will take it by force (velaqachti bekozek).” The use of force (kozek, power/violence) transforms a religious dispute into social coercion. This is the abusive-authority pattern condemned throughout the prophets: Amos 5:12 (those who take bribes and push the needy aside), Jeremiah 7:11 (the temple as a den of robbers). The Shiloh sanctuary becomes the prototype against which all subsequent temple corruption is measured.
17: “The sin of these young men was very great in the sight of the LORD, for they treated the LORD’s offering (minchat YHWH) with contempt (nave’atztu).” The verb nav’atztu (despised) is used in 2:30 for those who despise YHWH himself — the narrator equates contempt for YHWH’s offering with contempt for YHWH. This anticipates the NT principle that abuse of the Lord’s Supper constitutes sin “against the body and blood of the Lord” (1 Corinthians 11:27).
18: Samuel ministering before YHWH as a boy, clothed in a linen ephod (ephod bad) — the standard priestly vestment (2 Samuel 6:14: David wears one while dancing before the ark). The contrast with Eli’s sons is pointed: while they exploit the sanctuary, Samuel wears the garment of genuine service. The child dressed in priestly linen, devoted to YHWH before he can understand what that means, is a striking image of vocation preceding comprehension — as with John the Baptist leaping in the womb before cognitive awareness (Luke 1:44).
19: Hannah’s annual visit with a new little robe (me’il qatan) for Samuel. The me’il was a standard outer garment; its small size marks the child’s age and growth. The annual visit becomes a recurring liturgical act of reconnection: each year Hannah brings the robe as a token of her ongoing commitment to the vow. The garment grows larger each year as Samuel grows. The mother who gave her son away to God continues to clothe him — a beautiful image of ongoing maternal love within the structure of dedication.
20: Eli’s annual blessing: “May the LORD give you children by this woman in return for the gift (ha-she’ilah) she has given to the LORD.” The blessing reverses the expected economics of vows: normally you give something to get something. Here, the gift-given leads to more gifts received. Consecration is not a zero-sum transaction but an economics of abundance — giving to God generates more giving. Abraham’s willingness to give Isaac leads to the oath of blessing (Genesis 22:16-18).
21: The LORD visited (paqad) Hannah — the same verb used for YHWH’s redemptive action with Sarah (Genesis 21:1) and for the Exodus promise (Exodus 3:16: “I have surely visited you”). Hannah bore three more sons and two daughters — six children in all. Samuel, meanwhile, “grew up in the presence of the LORD (im YHWH).” The phrase im YHWH signals intimate divine presence. Luke 2:52 uses similar language of Jesus: “And Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man.”
22: Eli was very old and heard about everything his sons did — including that they were “sleeping with the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting (ohel mo’ed).” The tent of meeting reference uses the tabernacle terminology for the Shiloh sanctuary, connecting it to the Mosaic institution. The sexual misconduct of priests at the sanctuary recalls the Canaanite fertility cult practices explicitly condemned throughout Deuteronomy. Eli’s sons have assimilated the worst of surrounding religious practice into YHWH’s sanctuary.
23: Eli’s rebuke is gentle: “Why do you do such things? For I hear of your evil deeds from all these people.” But the rebuke’s gentleness is itself condemned by the divine perspective: Eli says “I hear” but does nothing more than speak. The passivity of effective tolerance is judged as severely as the sons’ active sin. 1 Samuel 3:13 will specify YHWH’s indictment: “because his sons were blaspheming God and he did not rebuke them.” Parental/leadership passivity in the face of flagrant sin is not neutrality but complicity.
24: “It is not a good report (lo-tovah hashemuah) that I hear the LORD’s people spreading.” The concern is reputational: what people are saying. Eli’s rebuke prioritizes the public report over the moral reality. The same failure appears in John 12:42-43 where leaders who believed in Jesus would not confess him “for they loved human praise more than the praise of God.” Eli’s sons are not restrained because the rebuke is motivated by image-management rather than fidelity to YHWH.
25: “But they would not listen to the voice of their father, for it was the will of the LORD to put them to death (ki chaphetz YHWH lehavitam).” This is the hardening pattern of Pharaoh applied to Eli’s sons: their refusal to repent and the divine determination to judge them are intertwined. The question Eli poses — “if someone sins against the LORD, who will intercede for him (mi yitpallel-bo)?” — is answered implicitly: no one can. This raises the deepest theological question that the NT answers: who can intercede for those whose sin is against YHWH? The one unique intercessor, Jesus Christ (1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 7:25).
26: “The young man Samuel continued to grow and to be in favor (tov) with the LORD and with men.” This developmental summary deliberately contrasts Samuel’s flourishing with the degeneration of Eli’s house. The phrase gam im YHWH vegam im anashim (with YHWH and with men) is precisely echoed in Luke 2:52 about Jesus: “Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man.” Luke’s language is not coincidental — the Samuel narrative is the template for the Lukan presentation of Jesus’ childhood.
28: The man of God reviews the Aaronic priestly election: “I chose your ancestor from all the tribes of Israel to be my priest — to go up to my altar, to burn incense, and to wear the ephod before me.” The three priestly functions — altar service, incense, ephod (oracular consultation) — represent the full range of sanctuary ministry. The historical election of the Aaronide line (Exodus 28:1; Numbers 3:10) is rehearsed to establish the weight of the covenant being broken.
29: “Why then do you despise my sacrifices and my offerings... and honor your sons more than me by fattening yourselves on the best parts?” The accusation identifies the root sin: honoring sons above YHWH. Eli’s failure is not ignorance but misplaced honor. This is the corruption of nepotism in religious clothing — the very thing Jesus condemns in Matthew 15:4-6 when the Pharisees use “Corban” declarations to evade the commandment to honor parents: human religious arrangements used to circumvent divine commandments.
30: “Those who honor me I will honor, and those who despise me will be treated with contempt (yeqallu).” One of the clearest statements of divine reciprocity in the OT: the relationship between YHWH and his servants mirrors the relationship the servants maintain toward him. The revocability of dynastic promises based on covenant faithfulness applies to the Davidic dynasty (Psalms 89:30-32; 1 Kings 9:6-7) and is fulfilled when the entire priestly and royal system is replaced in Christ (Hebrews 7:11-12).
31: “The days are coming when I will cut off your strength and the strength of your father’s house, so that no man in your house will reach old age.” The cutting off of the arm (zerao’, arm = power/lineage) is a covenantal curse. Fulfilled when Doeg kills the priests of Nob (1 Samuel 22:18-19) and when Solomon removes Abiathar (1 Kings 2:26-27) — a fulfillment 1 Kings 2:27 explicitly notes: “to fulfill what the LORD had spoken concerning the house of Eli.”
32: “You will look with envy at all the good that will be done for Israel, but there will never be an old man in your house.” The house of Eli will witness Israel’s blessing from a position of irreversible exclusion. The pathos of covenant forfeiture: you see the abundance of what was available and know it has been squandered. The same structure appears when Israel watches Gentiles receive covenant status (Romans 11:11: “through their trespass salvation has come to the Gentiles, so as to make Israel jealous”).
33: One surviving member of Eli’s house will be left at YHWH’s altar, but only to “consume your eyes with grief and break your heart (le-dakke’ot et-einekha uleda’avon et-napshekha).” The Hebrew dakke’ot einekha is an intense expression — literally “to cause your eyes to languish/waste away.” The sight of what could have been is more painful than ignorance of it.
34: “This will be the sign (ha-ot) for you: both your sons, Hophni and Phinehas, shall die on the same day.” A sign-in-advance that the oracle is reliable. Fulfilled in 1 Samuel 4:11 when both sons die during the battle of Aphek and the Philistines capture the ark. The precision of the sign underlines YHWH’s foreknowledge and control of events (cf. Deuteronomy 18:21-22: fulfilled prophecy as the criterion of true prophethood).
36: The prophecy ends with the humiliation of Eli’s surviving descendants: they will beg the new faithful priest for “a piece of silver or a loaf of bread” just to serve in a minor priestly function. The reversal of their earlier wealth (fattening on the best portions, 1 Samuel 2:29) into begging for bread is the perfect structural inversion. The oracle thus ends where Hannah’s song began: with reversal. The faithful priest who will replace Eli’s house is initially fulfilled in Zadok (replacing Abiathar under Solomon) and ultimately in Christ, the eternal high priest (Hebrews 5:5-6; 1 Samuel 7:11-17).
